metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit acknowledgment in the parent or a child (e.g., 0f70 or c1de) that SC1 coverage is conditional on corpus enrichment state, and specify a minimum corpus coverage fraction (or a 'corpus warm' gate) for the feature to be considered operationally meeting SC1.
findings.finding: Parent SC1 states overlap is detected against 'every non-graph-excluded ticket in the store', but child 5a8f explicitly skips corpus tickets with absent or stale digests, and the enrichment queue has a 60-minute soak window plus async drain latency. No child acknowledges this as a carve-out of SC1, and no child measures or bounds the fraction of tickets that may be cold at query time. The parent's success criterion is structurally unachievable at cold-start without an explicit acknowledgment and remediation path.
findings.location: child 5a8f-d6a0-ca32-4f51 (Cold/stale section) vs parent SC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to 0f70's advisory output when skip_count > 0 indicating the fraction of corpus covered, and document in the ADR that SC1 is met 'for the enriched fraction of the corpus' with full coverage achieved after initial drain completion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between SC1 ('every non-graph-excluded ticket') and the async/opportunistic enrichment design by either (a) weakening SC1 to 'every ticket whose digest is cached at review time' and adding a coverage-ratio floor AC, or (b) specifying a synchronous fallback path for tickets with missing digests.
findings.finding: SC1 claims overlap is detected against 'every non-graph-excluded ticket in the store', but the Design specifies that digest enrichment is async and drained 'opportunistically', meaning some tickets may lack a cached Cupid digest at review time. There is no stated fallback, partial-coverage rule, or degradation criterion, so SC1 cannot be satisfied when the queue is partially drained — a direct contradiction between the success criterion and the async design.
findings.location: Success Criteria item 1 vs. Design section ('Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)')
findings.suggested_fix: Amend SC1 to read: 'Overlap is detected against every non-graph-excluded ticket whose Cupid digest is cached at review time; tickets pending enrichment are skipped and logged. Coverage ratio (cached digests / total eligible tickets) must exceed X% on the S6 fixture.' Add a corresponding AC: 'When a digest is absent, the tool emits a warning listing unchecked tickets and exits cleanly without blocking the review.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit total landing order for all config.py edits (ee3d → 2d0f → e1f4 → 5a8f → 9022 → c1de → 0f70 or similar), and add a test that enumerates all expected LLMConfig fields by name so any accidentally dropped from_env() wiring fails loudly.
findings.finding: Four sibling children (ee3d, 5a8f, 9022, c1de) all independently add new fields and helpers to the same src/rebar/llm/config.py file's LLMConfig dataclass and from_env() function. The landing order for _llm_float is partially specified (5a8f before 9022), but the full ordering across all four stories is not specified, and no child acknowledges the merge-conflict risk on the from_env() return cls(...) call, which all four stories extend simultaneously.
findings.location: child ee3d-fc7b-e280-4cbb (Scope section), child 5a8f-d6a0-ca32-4f51 (Config section), child 9022-d3e3-e97f-47e3 (Config section), child c1de-d6a0-6cef-4135 (Scope section)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test_llm_config_all_fields_wired that sets all REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_* env vars and calls LLMConfig.from_env(), asserting each field resolves to the env value (not the default). This catches any from_env() merge-conflict drop. Specify the full config.py landing order in the parent epic or a shared integration note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the initial 793-ticket bulk backfill is idempotent and resumable: define a 'skip-if-digest-exists-and-valid' guard (e.g., hash of ticket text matches stored digest header) so interrupted runs re-start from the first unprocessed ticket without re-writing valid entries.
findings.finding: The initial bulk backfill of all 793 existing tickets into the Cupid digest cache is not described as resumable or idempotent. If enrichment is interrupted mid-run (process crash, API quota error, etc.), the plan does not state whether already-processed tickets are skipped on re-run or re-processed (potentially double-spending LLM cost or producing inconsistent cache state).
findings.location: Design section — 'Enrichment economics: VALIDATED. ~$0.0024/ticket, ≈ $1.90 one-time for all 793 tickets, cached and re-run only on edit'; Tier 1 drain description
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S1/the enrichment-queue spec: 'A digest is written atomically (tmp-file + rename); before writing, if a digest already exists and its source-hash matches the current `_ticket_text` hash, the ticket is skipped. The bulk-backfill loop iterates the full store but short-circuits on cache-hit, making the pass fully resumable at zero extra LLM cost.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the 'own graph' exclusion boundary precisely: state which graph primitives are used (e.g., direct `depends_on`/`supersedes`/`duplicates` links from the `deps` field, plus same-epic parent/children from the ticket hierarchy) and whether the traversal is one-hop or transitive — so implementers know exactly which ticket ids are excluded from the BM25F candidate set.
findings.finding: The 'graph exclusion' boundary — 'excluding the query ticket's own graph' — is not defined precisely enough for implementers to determine which tickets are excluded from the candidate set, creating a mechanism ambiguity that directly affects which overlaps are surfaced vs silently suppressed.
findings.location: Design section Stage (1): 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates, excluding the query ticket's own graph'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the S3 vs S4 numbering: confirm that 0f70's 'depends_on S3' refers to e1f4 (the queue story), update both 0f70 and c1de to use a consistent identifier (ticket ID or codename), and verify the landing order constraint is unambiguous.
findings.finding: 0f70 and c1de both reference an 'S3' story that edits plan_review/__init__.py (the certification/enqueue path), on which 0f70 declares a depends_on. However, c1de labels e1f4 as 'S4 (vegan-forgetful-tiger)', and no child in the 7-child roster is explicitly labeled 'S3'. If S3 is a codename for e1f4 (the queue story), the dependency is on e1f4; if S3 is a separate unlisted story, 0f70's depends_on is on a non-existent sibling.
findings.location: child 0f70-6d61-2b78-4c34 (Dependencies section) and child c1de-d6a0-6cef-4135 (Dependencies section)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'depends_on S3' in 0f70 with 'depends_on e1f4 (the enrichment queue story)' and note that e1f4 must land before 0f70 to avoid a merge conflict on plan_review/__init__.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan: specify that setting `overlap_enabled=false` fully suppresses queue enqueues AND digest cache writes (not just surface output), and describe how to drain or purge an in-progress queue and remove accumulated digest sidecars if the feature must be rolled back.
findings.finding: The plan modifies `_config_schema.py` (adding `overlap_enabled` to `VerifyConfig` and new tunables to `LLMConfig`) and introduces persistent sidecar digest writes to the event store via an enrichment queue, but provides no explicit rollback/back-out plan. The `overlap_enabled` flag is mentioned as a disable switch but the plan does not confirm it fully suppresses all side effects (queue enqueues, digest cache writes). If the feature causes problems in production, there is no specified mechanism to cleanly remove or roll back accumulated digests or drain a partially-processed queue.
findings.location: Design section — 'event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)' and 'each ticket gets a cached, LLM-extracted Cupid digest'; Config placement section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section, add a 'Rollback path' paragraph: (1) confirm `overlap_enabled=false` is a complete kill-switch that prevents both queue enqueues and digest writes; (2) provide a CLI command or migration note (e.g., `rebar overlap purge-digests`) to remove cached digests; (3) state that the enrichment queue drain is idempotent and can be safely abandoned by clearing the queue file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider adding a thin synchronous-digest fast path (extract digest inline at review time, bypass the async queue) as an early vertical slice that delivers observable end-to-end advisory output sooner, deferring the async enrichment queue/drain to a later child or tier.
findings.finding: The 7-child dependency chain (digest-extract → digest-persist → enqueue → drain → BM25F → judge → wire) means no working end-to-end slice exists until most children ship; a synchronous-digest fast path (extract on demand, skip async queue/drain for the first vertical slice) could deliver a reviewable end-to-end overlap result earlier and defer the async enrichment infrastructure to a follow-on child or tier.
findings.location: Design section — 'Two-stage retrieve-then-rerank' + child list S1–S7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) declare rebar's overlap detector POSIX-only (matching the existing `lock.py` constraint, which imports `fcntl` directly) and drop the Windows os.name guard claim, or (b) live-test the Windows guard path before calling the store-primitive 'CONFIRMED' for the queue use-case.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the existing write-lock (`lock.acquire`/`LockHandle`, `txn.py` precedent) 'supports the queue's read-check-append AND atomic enqueue' and that 'Windows detach requires an `os.name` guard (API-derisked, not live-tested).' The existing `src/rebar/_store/lock.py` uses `import fcntl` (POSIX-only; confirmed in source) and the guard is explicitly acknowledged as not live-tested. For a new enrichment queue that reuses this lock on Windows, the capability assertion — that the write-lock is usable at all on Windows, even behind a guard — is unverified beyond an API-level desk check.
findings.location: Design section — 'Store primitives: CONFIRMED. the manual write-lock (lock.acquire/LockHandle, txn.py precedent) supports the queue's read-check-append AND atomic enqueue; … Windows detach requires an os.name guard (API-derisked, not live-tested)'
findings.suggested_fix: Since `lock.py` already uses POSIX-only `fcntl`, the simplest resolution is to declare the overlap detector POSIX-only and remove the 'Windows detach requires an os.name guard' hedge — it cannot be derisked at the API level when the underlying lock is `fcntl`-only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a batching/throttling constraint for the initial bulk-enrichment pass (e.g., max N concurrent enrichment calls, or a configurable `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_BATCH_SIZE` / inter-call delay) so the one-time backfill does not fire all 793 API calls simultaneously.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the initial bulk backfill is batched/throttled or executed as a single unthrottled sweep. For 793 tickets this is a real but bounded LLM API risk (rate limits, cost spike), and the plan's silence means child stories have no constraint to implement throttling.
findings.location: Design section — 'Enrichment economics: VALIDATED. ≈ $1.90 one-time for all 793 tickets'; Tier-1 drain description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the post-rollback schema state for digest cache files and the event queue: assert whether old code ignores unknown-version digests (forward-compatible reads), whether stale queue entries are drained or abandoned on rollback, and confirm no data loss on a mid-write crash (atomic write pattern already cited — make this explicit in the spec).
findings.finding: The plan is silent on the rollback / back-out path for the digest cache and event-sourced queue. If a deployment is rolled back after partial enrichment, the plan does not assert the schema state of these persisted artefacts post-rollback (are stale digests safe to leave in place? is the queue drained or replayed?), and does not guarantee no data loss on partial failure of an enqueue-or-drain operation.
findings.location: Design section — 'event-sourced queue drained opportunistically'; 'digest-as-REVIEW_RESULT-style-sidecar'; Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria (all silent on rollback)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback posture' sub-section: 'Digest files carry a `schema_version` header; readers skip files with unrecognised versions (forward-compatible). Queue entries are append-only and drained lazily; rolled-back code simply stops draining — entries accumulate harmlessly and are replayed when the feature is re-deployed. Mid-write crashes are safe because digests are written via tmp-file + atomic rename; a missing digest triggers re-enrichment on next plan-review invocation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test (or documented guarantee) that the maybe_drain soaked? check is O(1) or bounded regardless of queue size and store size, and add a regression test for a non-overlap write command (e.g., rebar create) to verify it completes within its normal latency budget with maybe_drain injected.
findings.finding: c1de injects maybe_drain() into event_append.py on the hot write path for ALL write commands system-wide, not only overlap-related ones. No child tests or bounds the impact of this injection on non-overlap write commands (e.g., rebar create, rebar update, rebar link, rebar claim). The 20ms gate budget reduces but does not eliminate the risk: if the soaked? check itself is slow (e.g., it loads queue reducer state on a large store), every write command degrades.
findings.location: child c1de-d6a0-6cef-4135 (maybe_drain injection point section)
findings.suggested_fix: In c1de, add test_gate_latency_with_backlog that seeds a store with N soaked entries and asserts maybe_drain's gate check completes in <DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS ms. Also wrap maybe_drain in a try/except in event_append.py so any exception is logged and swallowed, never propagating to the write command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State numeric pass/fail thresholds for SC1/AC1 (e.g., recall@20 ≥ 0.9 and precision ≥ 0.7 on the S6 labeled fixture) so the criterion is evaluable in-session without subjective judgment.
findings.finding: Success Criteria 1 and AC 1 reference the S6 labeled precision/recall fixture for measurement but state no numeric pass/fail threshold (e.g., recall@20 ≥ X, precision ≥ Y). 'Measured by the S6 labeled precision/recall fixture' and 'detected… (delivered across S1–S7)' are process-framing, not evaluable outcomes — an executor cannot determine whether the implementation passes without a stated target.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 1; Acceptance Criteria bullet 1
findings.suggested_fix: Append to SC1: '…fixture; pass threshold: recall@20 ≥ 0.9 AND precision ≥ 0.7 on the labeled pair set.' Mirror the same threshold in AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default conf_threshold value (e.g., 0.7) in the parent plan's Config placement section so child implementers have a consistent starting point rather than each choosing independently.
findings.finding: The 'bounded LLM pairwise judge' is described as bounded but the parent plan states no default for conf_threshold (the bounding parameter), leaving an executor to choose an appropriate cutoff value when implementing or integrating the judge.
findings.location: Design section, paragraph 1: 'a bounded LLM pairwise judge classifies each candidate'; Config placement section: 'conf_threshold' listed as a tunable
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Config placement: 'Default: conf_threshold=0.7 (surface only candidates where the judge's confidence exceeds 70%); tunable via REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate a user-facing location (inline `_config_schema.py` comments, a docs/ config-reference page, or a dedicated section in the ADR) that documents each `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env var and `overlap_enabled` with their type, default, valid range, and effect.
findings.finding: The new `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` environment-variable tunables and `overlap_enabled` config key are named in the plan but their valid values, defaults, and semantics are not designated for any user-facing config reference documentation. The ADR (S7) is scoped to architectural decisions, not configuration reference, leaving operators without a documented source of truth for these tunables.
findings.location: Config placement section; Success Criteria / AC — 'The architecture is captured in an ADR under docs/adr/ (owned by S7)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the S7 ADR to include a 'Configuration Reference' appendix listing each tunable (name, type, default, valid range, effect), or add inline docstrings/comments to the `LLMConfig` and `VerifyConfig` schema definitions in `_config_schema.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the enrichment queue's lock protocol: explicitly state that (a) the ENQUEUE operation (read-check-append to the queue file) runs under a brief write-lock acquisition, and (b) the DRAIN/LLM-enrichment operation runs OUTSIDE the write lock — with the queue entry marked 'in-flight' or removed before releasing the lock, so concurrent drainers don't double-enrich.
findings.finding: The enrichment queue's lock protocol conflates the ENQUEUE operation (brief, safe to hold the write lock) with the DRAIN operation (slow LLM enrichment, must NOT hold the write lock), leaving a mechanism gap: if the drain runs under the unified write lock it would block the entire ticket store for the duration of LLM calls; if it doesn't, the atomicity guarantee the plan credits to the write lock only covers enqueue, not drain — and the plan never distinguishes these two operations' lock protocols.
findings.location: Design section: 'Store primitives: CONFIRMED. the manual write-lock (lock.acquire/LockHandle, txn.py precedent) supports the queue's read-check-append AND atomic enqueue'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify a two-phase queue protocol: (1) under the write lock: read queue, skip if ticket already present or in-flight, append a 'pending' entry, release; (2) outside the write lock: run LLM enrichment; (3) under the write lock again: replace 'pending' with the completed digest or remove on failure. This matches the existing txn.py pattern (lock → read → write → commit → release) and never holds the unified write lock during LLM calls.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific Python library (e.g. `rank_bm25`, `bm25s`, or a custom implementation) that provides BM25F, confirm its field-weighted scoring variant matches the experiment setup, and declare it as a dependency in the appropriate extra in `pyproject.toml` before S1 begins.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests' as its retrieval engine throughout, but never names the Python library or confirms whether BM25F will be implemented from scratch. `rank_bm25` (the canonical Python BM25/BM25F implementation) is neither mentioned as the prior-art choice nor excluded with a reason; it does not appear in pyproject.toml or any source file. If `rank_bm25` (or another library) is the vehicle, it must be added as a dependency (to the `[agents]` extra or a new optional) and its BM25F variant confirmed to support field-weighted scoring. If BM25F is being implemented from scratch, that is significant engineering scope not called out in the plan. This is a load-bearing unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: Design section — 'Two-stage retrieve-then-rerank: … (1) brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates'; Derisking experiments — 'digest-BM25F retrieved 8/8 heavy paraphrases at rank 1, recall@20 = 1.0'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Design section: 'BM25F is provided by `<library>` (added to the `[agents]` extra / a new `[overlap]` optional); the `BM25Okapi`/`BM25L`/BM25F class is used with field weights [title, description, AC].' This resolves T1(c) and pins the prior-art consideration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the BM25F implementation vehicle (e.g., custom pure-Python scorer, specific library with BM25F field-weighting support) and reconcile it with the 'no new dependency' claim — or explicitly permit a new optional library dep and update pyproject.toml accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'NO new dependency' for BM25F retrieval, but BM25F (field-weighted BM25) is not available in the Python stdlib, not in any declared dependency in pyproject.toml, and not implemented anywhere in the codebase. The derisking experiments validated recall of 'digest-BM25F' but the experiment scripts lived under /tmp (no repo files touched), leaving the implementation vehicle — new library (e.g. `rank_bm25`, `whoosh`) vs. custom pure-Python — completely unspecified. The `rank_bm25` library (the most common choice) provides BM25Okapi/BM25L/BM25Plus but NOT BM25F field weighting; implementing BM25F from scratch is ~100–200 lines of non-trivial scoring logic. Without naming the implementation vehicle, the 'no new dependency' claim and the 'field-weighted BM25F' capability claim are in direct tension, and the children have no grounded implementation path.
findings.location: Design section — Stage 1 (candidate generation): 'brute-force BM25F "More Like This"'; Derisking experiments: 'field-weighted BM25F over digests'; 'NO embeddings, NO vector DB, NO new dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a new optional extra (e.g., `overlap = ["rank-bm25f"]` or implement a lightweight pure-Python BM25F scorer inline in the feature module and document it), or (b) relax the 'no new dependency' claim to 'no new CORE dependency' and add a guarded optional dep to the `agents` extra. State the choice explicitly in the Design section so all children have a grounded implementation path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the pairwise judge's CLOSED relation enum ({duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}), the both-orderings aggregation rule, and the fail-safe-toward-distinct semantics in the parent epic's Design section (or explicitly cross-reference S6 as the normative spec), so an implementer has the full schema co-located with the system description.
findings.finding: The parent plan's Design section names 'a bounded LLM pairwise judge classifies each candidate' but does not define the CLOSED 5-value relation enum {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated} or any processing/aggregation protocol (double-ordering, direction-consistency rule, fail-safe-toward-distinct). These are only specified in child S6 (faint-ethnic-merganser). An implementer reading only the epic plan has no vocabulary to implement the judge's output schema.
findings.location: Design section — 'a bounded LLM pairwise judge classifies each candidate and surfaces ≤3 advisory link suggestions'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Judge output schema (normative)' subsection to the Design section that reproduces the 5-value closed enum, the double-ordering requirement, the direction-consistency aggregation rule, and the abstain-drops-without-consuming-a-slot rule — or replace the current sentence with an explicit 'see S6 (faint-ethnic-merganser) for the normative output schema and aggregation protocol'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the Cupid digest field schema (at minimum: field names, types, and which fields BM25F weights apply to) in the parent epic's Design section, so S1/S2/S5/S6 share a common contract.
findings.finding: The plan names a 'Cupid digest' as the cached, LLM-extracted per-ticket artifact that BM25F runs over, but provides zero field-level specification of the digest schema anywhere in the parent plan — no fields, no extraction prompt vocabulary, no constraints. This schema is the foundational input to the BM25F retrieval stage and the judge's `a_digest`/`b_digest` inputs, yet it is undefined at the plan level.
findings.location: Design section — '(0) each ticket gets a cached, LLM-extracted Cupid digest (no embeddings, no vector DB)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the parent epic's Design section a summary of the `overlap_judge.md` prompt's required encoding (the CLOSED 5-value relation set, the shared-artifact gate, the both-orderings protocol, and the abstain criterion) so the judge's instruction content is specified at the epic level, not only in the child story.
findings.finding: The parent plan references 'overlap_judge.md' rubric prompt (implicitly, via the judge description) as the mechanism that encodes the classification vocabulary, shared-artifact requirement, and abstain criterion, but provides no content specification for this prompt in the parent plan. The rubric body is load-bearing (it is the only instruction a single-turn LLM call receives) yet is entirely unspecified at the epic level, and the file does not exist in the codebase.
findings.location: Design section — 'a bounded LLM pairwise judge classifies each candidate'; S6 scope: 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md … whose body MUST encode'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction: either ee3d's enrich.py DOES import and use gates.ticket_text() for its source text (accepting the cross-package import), or 2d0f's content_hash is computed over enrich.py's exact inline join — both stories must agree on a single canonical normalizer for the content_hash freshness signal.
findings.finding: ee3d explicitly states enrich.py uses its own inline text join and does NOT import gates._ticket_text, but 2d0f states 'both this story and S5 (enrich) import the public name [ticket_text()]' — a direct contradiction. If these are different normalizations, the content_hash stored in the TICKET_DIGEST sidecar (computed via gates.ticket_text()) diverges from the text fed to the LLM (enrich.py's inline join), making freshness checks unreliable: a digest produced from enrich's join could be mis-marked stale because the hash was computed over a different join.
findings.location: child ee3d-fc7b-e280-4cbb (Op signature section) vs child 2d0f-19f2-dd15-4336 (Content hash section)
findings.suggested_fix: Have 2d0f compute the content_hash by hashing the same text that enrich.py actually feeds to the LLM. The simplest resolution: promote gates._ticket_text to public (as 2d0f proposes) and have enrich.py import and use it too, making a single canonical normalizer — remove ee3d's inline join and align both stories on the public ticket_text() call.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
